title: How to Build an Ethical Fashion Empire Without Burning Out
guestBio: Kathryn Letson is the founder of By The Sea Company, an ethical fashion brand that has grown to serve over 3,000 retailers. After experiencing burnout in NYC's high-pressure fashion industry, she pivoted to create a sustainable business model rooted in ergonomic design and eco-conscious textiles from her base in Kauai.
excerpt: Kathryn Letson turned her corporate fashion burnout into a breakthrough, building By The Sea Company into an ethical fashion empire with over 3,000 retailers. Discover how she created a business that aligns with her values while prioritizing wellbeing over hustle culture.
metaDescription: Learn how Kathryn Letson built an ethical fashion empire with 3,000+ retailers after NYC burnout. Tips for sustainable entrepreneurship and conscious business.
keyTakeaways: Burnout can become the catalyst for creating a business model that truly fits your life and values.
Ethical fashion means considering the entire supply chain, from materials to manufacturing to worker conditions.
You don't have to sacrifice your wellbeing to build something significant in business.
Starting small at farmers markets can be a powerful way to test and refine your business concept.
Living in alignment with your environment can significantly impact your creativity and leadership style.
Building a sustainable business requires designing systems that support both profit and principles.
The future of fashion lies in innovative, eco-conscious textiles that don't compromise on style or function.

pullQuotes.text: You don't have to sacrifice your wellbeing to build something big.

timestamps.label: First Steps in Launching By The Sea Company
Life in Kauai and Creative Leadership

content:
What if the breakdown you're avoiding is actually the breakthrough you need? In this powerful conversation with Kathryn Letson, founder of By The Sea Company, we explore how corporate burnout became the foundation for building an ethical fashion empire that serves over 3,000 retailers worldwide.

Kathryn's story begins in the high-pressure fashion houses of New York City, where the relentless pace and unsustainable practices left her questioning everything she thought she knew about success. Like so many of us, she found herself caught in the trap of believing that bigger, faster, and harder were the only paths to building something meaningful. But her body and spirit had other plans.

When Burnout Becomes Your Business Plan

The turning point came when Kathryn realized she could create her own template for success. Instead of continuing to pour herself into systems that drained her, she chose to design a business model that actually fit her life and values. This wasn't about scaling back her ambitions—it was about scaling them in a direction that made sense.

The move from Manhattan to Kauai wasn't just a change of scenery; it was a complete reimagining of what entrepreneurship could look like. Surrounded by the natural beauty of Hawaii, Kathryn began to understand how environment shapes creativity and leadership. The slower pace didn't mean less productivity—it meant more intentional productivity.

Starting at local farmers markets, she tested her concept of ergonomic design meets eco-conscious fashion. These weren't just sales opportunities; they were research labs where she could connect directly with customers and refine her vision. The feedback was immediate and honest, helping her build a foundation based on real needs rather than industry assumptions.

Defining Ethical Fashion in Action

One of the most eye-opening parts of this conversation centers on what ethical fashion actually means beyond the buzzwords. Kathryn breaks down the complexity of creating truly sustainable clothing, from sourcing eco-conscious textiles to ensuring fair labor practices throughout the supply chain.

This isn't about perfection—it's about progress and transparency. By The Sea Company's approach demonstrates that you can build a profitable business while prioritizing both people and planet. The key is being willing to do the hard work of researching suppliers, investing in quality materials, and educating customers about why these choices matter.

The ergonomic design element adds another layer to the sustainability conversation. When clothing is designed for comfort and movement, people wear it longer and more frequently. This challenges the fast fashion model not through shame or restriction, but by creating pieces so comfortable and versatile that customers naturally gravitate toward quality over quantity.

Building Systems That Support Your Values

What strikes me most about Kathryn's approach is how she's managed to scale her business without scaling her stress. Growing to over 3,000 retailers requires sophisticated systems and processes, but she's maintained the core principles that guided her from those early farmers market days.

This is where so many entrepreneurs get stuck—they assume that growth requires abandoning the values and practices that made their business special in the first place. Kathryn proves that the opposite is true. By staying committed to her principles around sustainability, worker welfare, and design excellence, she's created a brand that retailers and customers trust.

The geographic element plays a crucial role here too. Operating from Kauai forces a different relationship with time and urgency. While her business serves customers globally, her daily rhythm is grounded in island time, which creates space for reflection and strategic thinking that's often lost in the corporate hustle.

The Future of Conscious Commerce

Perhaps the most exciting part of this conversation is Kathryn's vision for the future of textiles and fabric innovation. She's not just participating in the sustainable fashion movement—she's helping to shape it. From exploring new eco-friendly materials to pioneering manufacturing processes that reduce environmental impact, By The Sea Company is proving that innovation and ethics can work hand in hand.

This forward-thinking approach extends to how she views business growth itself. Instead of the traditional model of expanding at all costs, she's focused on deepening relationships with existing retail partners and continuously improving product quality. This creates a more stable, sustainable business model that benefits everyone in the supply chain.

The lesson here isn't that everyone needs to move to a tropical island to build an ethical business. It's that we all have permission to question the conventional wisdom about how businesses should operate and what success should look like. Kathryn's journey from NYC burnout to ethical fashion leader demonstrates that when we align our work with our values, we create space for both personal fulfillment and professional impact.

As you listen to this conversation, I invite you to consider where your own burnout signals might be pointing toward breakthrough opportunities. What would it look like to design a business or career that supports your wellbeing while creating meaningful change in the world? Kathryn's story reminds us that we don't have to choose between success and sustainability—in fact, the most powerful businesses are often built when we refuse to make that compromise.

quiz.title: What's Your Sustainable Success Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach building something meaningful without burning out. Your results will reveal your unique path to creating impact while staying true to your values.

questions.question: When you're feeling overwhelmed at work, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Question whether the work itself aligns with what you really care about
Look for ways to systematize or streamline your current processes
Consider what needs to change or shift to make things more manageable
Step back and assess how this is affecting your overall life balance

questions.question: When starting a new project, you typically:

options.text: Research the deeper impact and ethical implications first
Create a detailed timeline and plan before moving forward
Start with what you have and adapt as you learn
Consider how it fits with your current life and energy levels

questions.question: Your definition of business success includes:

options.text: Making a positive difference in the world through your work
Building something stable and sustainable for the long term
Having the flexibility to evolve and respond to opportunities
Maintaining your health and relationships while growing professionally

questions.question: When you hear about someone's rapid business growth, you think:

options.text: I wonder what corners they might have cut to get there so fast
That's great for them, but I prefer building methodically
I'm curious about what strategies I could adapt for my situation
I hope they're not sacrificing their wellbeing for those results
Reflect your values and allow you to do meaningful work
Have clear structures and systems that support efficient progress
Offer variety and room to pivot based on what's working
Support your whole self—creativity, relationships, and personal growth

questions.question: When facing a major life transition, you're most likely to:

options.text: Use it as an opportunity to realign with your core values
Plan carefully and take measured steps toward your new direction
Embrace the change and see where it leads you
Consider how to make the transition in a way that honors your whole life

results.title: The Conscious Pioneer
results.description: You're driven by purpose and aren't afraid to challenge the status quo when something doesn't align with your values. You naturally think about the bigger picture and long-term impact of your decisions. Sometimes you worry that prioritizing ethics means sacrificing growth, but you're learning that authentic success takes time. This episode will show you exactly how to build something significant while staying true to your principles—Kathryn's journey proves you don't have to choose between impact and integrity.

results.title: The Intentional Builder
results.description: You believe in steady, thoughtful growth over flashy overnight success. You're naturally drawn to creating systems and processes that can sustain long-term success. You sometimes question if you're moving fast enough compared to others, but your methodical approach is actually your superpower. Kathryn's story of starting at farmers markets and building to 3,000+ retailers will inspire you to trust your measured approach and see how intentional growth creates lasting impact.

results.title: The Adaptive Innovator
results.description: You're incredibly resourceful and know how to pivot when life demands it. You've probably had to reinvent yourself before and you're not afraid of change when it serves your growth. You sometimes wonder if you're being too reactive instead of proactive, but your flexibility is a rare gift in business. This episode will resonate deeply as Kathryn shares how her biggest breakdown became her breakthrough—showing you how to turn life's curveballs into your competitive advantage.

results.title: The Holistic Harmonizer
results.description: You instinctively know that true success includes your wellbeing, relationships, and personal fulfillment—not just business metrics. You're always seeking balance and integration rather than compartmentalization. You might worry that your need for harmony makes you less ambitious, but you're actually ahead of the curve. Kathryn's transition from Manhattan burnout to building her empire from Kauai will speak directly to your soul and validate that your whole-life approach to success is exactly what the world needs.

I'm honored to meet you. >> A thank you and I love how our worlds collided at the Wipaw Farmers Market and I'm so glad you're here. So, let's start at the beginning. I'm cla a classically trained fashion designer who graduated from Parsons in New York City and was scouted immediately by Ralph Lauren and Donna Karen and people like that and so ended up working um with with Ralph Lauren for a little bit and then went off to Abberrombie and Fitch later, Gap later. I started Abbercrombie Girls with another woman which was an exciting project too. So, I had some very um big projects back in the day which were um amazing because they taught me from start to finish how to create something. Oh, what a cool opportunity to have something like that. That's so I mean it's kind of amazing you I when you me when you mentioned that you were in high fashion in New York and now I meet you on the north shore of Kawaii and you're in your stand and you've got all your beautiful clothes and I'm thinking okay there I got to hear this story like how do you go from here to there and I get it cuz I all the women I talk to who have you know have worked in corporate you know the burnout is real and it usually happens right when you should be at the height of your career. Oh, seriously. I mean, it and and for me it did. >> It did. I had full burnout. Um, I was really sick with autoimmune stuff. Mhm. Happy autoimmune shoggins disease and like all these things. If my kid has this thing I want to go to, I just don't schedule my clients around that time. You know, you all of a sudden you take your life back. Your time >> because what do we really is it is it the money or is it the time? It's really the time. >> Yeah. >> You know, that's the most working for yourself. You own your time. >> When you work for someone else, you sold your time to them. you right now. You work for you. You decide like we decide to meet up right now. It's our time. >> Absolutely. And I feel like I'm a very lucky girl. I grew up had two very entrepreneurial parents and that was very much the model that was shown to me as a child, you know, was although I felt like my mother very much tried to work like a man and she would work herself so hard and try to emulate the hours that a man would put in and she would always end up very very sick. That was the model. >> That was a model, >> you know, like she didn't have a female role model saying, "No, take a break." >> Yeah. She didn't have a period today. Go rest. >> Right. Exactly. Oh, or your kids have a school play. Go. You should go to that. You're going to regret not going to that. >> That That right there is balance. And when you work for yourself, no one's telling you not to balance like, "Oh, I have to go take care of that now." You do. >> Yeah. Yeah, you do. And we're getting so much better at it, too.
